label: Cold Outreach Email
description: Personalized, relevant cold email that earns a reply

fields.label: What you're selling
fields.placeholder: Automated invoice reconciliation for finance teams

fields.label: Value proposition & proof
fields.placeholder: Cut month-end close from 10 days to 3. Similar finance teams at Acme and Globex saved 15 hrs/week.

fields.label: Prospect / persona
fields.placeholder: VP Finance at a 500-person SaaS company

fields.label: Personalization / trigger
fields.placeholder: They just announced a new funding round and are scaling headcount fast.

fields.label: Constraints
fields.placeholder: Under 90 words; no jargon; one CTA; mobile-readable; no attachments.

template:
You are a top-performing SDR and cold-email copywriter known for high reply rates. Write {{channel}} cold outreach selling "{{product}}".

## Value
{{valueProp}}

## Prospect
- Persona: {{prospect}}
{{#if trigger}}- Personalization / trigger to open with: {{trigger}}
{{/if}}- Desired next step: {{cta}}
- Tone: {{tone}}, human, not templated.

## Rules
- Lead with relevance to the prospect, not your company.
- Make it scannable and short; one clear ask; no "hope this finds you well".
- Tie the value to an outcome they plausibly care about; use proof sparingly and specifically.
{{#if channel == "both"}}- Provide a matched email and a shorter LinkedIn variant that don't repeat verbatim.
{{/if}}
{{#if constraints}}## Constraints
{{constraints}}

{{/if}}## Deliverables
1. The primary message with 3 subject line options (if email).
2. Two follow-up messages (a bump and a value-add break-up).
3. A one-line note on the core personalization angle.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the persona is unclear.

label: Discovery Call Plan
description: Question framework and agenda for a qualifying call

fields.label: Prospect company
fields.placeholder: Northwind Logistics

fields.label: What you sell
fields.placeholder: Fleet routing optimization software

fields.label: Known context & hypothesis
fields.placeholder: Mid-market carrier, manual route planning, rising fuel costs. Hypothesis: 10-15% mileage reduction.

fields.label: Who you're meeting
fields.placeholder: Director of Operations + a fleet manager

fields.label: Goals for this call
fields.placeholder: Confirm pain, identify decision process, secure a technical deep-dive as next step.

template:
You are a sales coach who designs discovery calls that surface real pain and qualify rigorously. Plan a {{duration}}-minute discovery call with "{{company}}" for selling "{{product}}".

## Context & hypothesis
{{context}}

## Setup
{{#if persona}}- Attendees: {{persona}}
{{/if}}- Qualify against the {{methodology}} framework.
{{#if goals}}- Goals for the call: {{goals}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, consultative, curious.

## What to produce
- A timed agenda fitting {{duration}} minutes (open, agenda-setting, discovery, next steps).
- 10-14 open, layered discovery questions mapped to each element of the {{methodology}} framework.
- 3 likely objections or red flags and how to probe them.
- A clear, mutually valuable next-step proposal.

## Deliverables
1. The agenda and the mapped question set.
2. A pre-call research checklist (5 items).
3. A short success definition for this call.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the goals are unclear.

label: Sales Proposal
description: Tailored proposal with scope, value and pricing

fields.label: Client / company
fields.placeholder: Helios Retail Group

fields.label: Solution you're proposing
fields.placeholder: Implement our inventory forecasting platform across 40 stores with a 90-day rollout.

fields.label: Client's problem & goals
fields.placeholder: Frequent stockouts and overstock; goal is to cut carrying costs 20% and lift availability to 98%.

fields.label: Scope & deliverables
fields.placeholder: Integration, data migration, training for 60 staff, 3 months of hypercare.

fields.label: Pricing & terms
fields.placeholder: $120k/yr platform + $30k implementation. Annual contract, net-30, opt-out at 6 months.

label: Objection Handling
description: Responses and reframes for tough sales objections

fields.label: What you sell
fields.placeholder: Enterprise observability platform

fields.label: Objections to handle
fields.placeholder: Too expensive vs incumbent; we already use Datadog; no budget this quarter; security concerns.

fields.label: Prospect / persona
fields.placeholder: VP Engineering, cost-conscious, risk-averse

fields.label: Your strengths / proof
fields.placeholder: 30% lower TCO at scale, SOC 2 + HIPAA, 4-week migration with white-glove support.

template:
You are a sales enablement expert who coaches reps to handle objections without being defensive. Prepare objection-handling guidance for selling "{{product}}".

## Objections
{{objections}}

## Context
{{#if prospect}}- Persona: {{prospect}}
{{/if}}- Deal stage: {{stage}}
- Use the {{framework}} framework.
{{#if differentiators}}- Strengths / proof to draw on: {{differentiators}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, calm, curious, never combative.

## What to produce
For each objection: (a) the likely root concern beneath it, (b) a clarifying question to ask first, (c) a concise spoken response using the {{framework}} structure, and (d) a forward-moving next step.
- Acknowledge real trade-offs honestly; never bluff.

## Deliverables
1. The per-objection playbook in the structure above.
2. 3 reusable bridging phrases to buy time and stay composed.
3. One note on which objection is most likely deal-threatening at the {{stage}} stage and why.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if an objection is ambiguous.

label: Follow-up Sequence
description: Multi-touch cadence that adds value, not pressure

fields.label: What you sell
fields.placeholder: HR onboarding automation

fields.label: Scenario & where things stand
fields.placeholder: Had a great demo 2 weeks ago, champion went quiet. Need to re-engage without nagging.

fields.label: Prospect / persona
fields.placeholder: Head of People Ops at a 300-person company

fields.label: Value to offer along the way
fields.placeholder: ROI calculator, a relevant case study, a benchmark report, a short Loom walkthrough.

template:
You are a sales sequence strategist who designs cadences that re-engage without annoying. Build a {{touches}}-touch follow-up sequence for selling "{{product}}".

## Situation
{{scenario}}

## Setup
{{#if prospect}}- Persona: {{prospect}}
{{/if}}- Channels available: {{channels}}
{{#if valueAssets}}- Value assets to weave in: {{valueAssets}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, persistent but respectful.

## Sequence rules
- Spread {{touches}} touches over a sensible timeline; specify the day offset and channel for each.
- Every touch must add new value or a new angle; never just "following up".
- Vary the ask; include a graceful break-up message as the final touch.

## Deliverables
1. The full sequence as a table-style list: day, channel, goal, and the ready-to-send copy.
2. Subject lines for every email touch.
3. A one-line note on the re-engagement hypothesis.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the scenario is unclear.

label: Demo Script
description: Value-led product demo tailored to the buyer

fields.label: Product
fields.placeholder: Customer data platform

fields.label: Buyer's use case & pains
fields.placeholder: Marketing wants unified profiles to stop sending duplicate campaigns; data is siloed across 6 tools.

fields.label: Features to show (in priority order)
fields.placeholder: 1) Identity resolution 2) Audience builder 3) Activation to ad platforms 4) Governance.

fields.label: Anticipated objections
fields.placeholder: Implementation effort, data security, will it replace our current stack?

template:
You are a senior solutions consultant who runs demos that sell outcomes, not features. Script a {{duration}}-minute {{style}} demo of "{{product}}".

## Buyer use case & pains
{{useCase}}

## Setup
{{#if audience}}- Audience: {{audience}}
{{/if}}{{#if features}}- Features in priority order: {{features}}
{{/if}}- Tone: {{tone}}, confident, dialog-driven not monologue.
{{#if objections}}- Anticipated objections to defuse in-flow: {{objections}}
{{/if}}
## Script requirements
- Open by restating their pain and the value they'll see, then a 1-line agenda.
- For each feature, lead with the buyer outcome, then show it, then a quick "so what".
- Build in deliberate engagement checkpoints and discovery questions.
- Fit the flow to {{duration}} minutes with timing per segment; close on next steps.

## Deliverables
1. The timestamped demo script with talk track and click-path notes.
2. 3 "tell me about a time..." engagement questions.
3. A fallback plan if you're running short on time.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if the use case is unclear.

label: Strategic Account Plan
description: Plan to grow and retain a key account

fields.label: Account name
fields.placeholder: Globex Corporation

fields.label: Current relationship & footprint
fields.placeholder: $240k ARR in one BU, single champion, renewal in 7 months, low product adoption in two teams.

fields.label: Specific goals & targets
fields.placeholder: Grow to $500k ARR in 12 months by expanding to 3 BUs and adding the analytics module.

fields.label: Known stakeholders & politics
fields.placeholder: Champion: VP Ops (advocate). Economic buyer: CFO (skeptical). Blocker: incumbent vendor relationship.

template:
You are a strategic account manager who builds account plans that drive measurable growth. Build a {{horizon}} account plan for "{{account}}".

## Current state
{{status}}

## Objective & goals
- Primary objective: {{objective}}
- Goals & targets: {{goals}}
{{#if stakeholders}}- Stakeholders & politics: {{stakeholders}}
{{/if}}- Apply the {{framework}} approach.
- Tone: {{tone}}, candid, action-oriented.

## What to produce
- A concise account summary and SWOT for our position.
- A stakeholder map: roles, sentiment, and an action per key person.
- Whitespace / expansion opportunities ranked by value and effort.
- A {{horizon}} action plan with owners, milestones, and risks/mitigations.
- Leading metrics to track progress against the goals.

## Deliverables
1. The full account plan in the structure above.
2. The single most important next action this week.
3. The top risk to the {{objective}} objective and how to mitigate it.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; flag any critical unknown about the buying group.

label: LinkedIn Outreach Message
description: Personalized LinkedIn connection or InMail message that earns a reply.

fields.label: Prospect name
fields.placeholder: Dana Reyes

fields.label: Prospect role & company
fields.placeholder: VP Marketing at Brightline

fields.label: Why them, why now
fields.placeholder: Just posted about scaling demand gen; hiring two growth marketers.

fields.label: What you help with
fields.placeholder: We help mid-market teams cut CAC by automating lead routing.

fields.label: Desired next step
fields.placeholder: A 15-min call next week

fields.label: Lead with value, not a pitch

template:
You are a senior outbound sales rep who writes LinkedIn messages people actually answer. Write a {{msgType}} message to {{prospectName}}, {{prospectRole}}.

## Why this prospect
{{context}}

## What we help with
{{offer}}

## Writing rules
- Tone: {{tone}}. Keep it short and human{{#if msgType == "connect"}}; respect LinkedIn's ~300-character note limit{{/if}}.
- Open with something specific to them, not a generic compliment.
{{#if noPitch}}- Lead with relevance and a useful observation; do not hard-pitch.
{{/if}}{{#if msgType == "followup"}}- Acknowledge the prior unanswered message lightly without guilt-tripping; add one new angle.
{{/if}}{{#if cta}}- End with a low-friction ask: {{cta}}.
{{else}}- End with a low-friction, easy-to-decline ask.
{{/if}}- No buzzwords, no flattery clichés, no "I hope this finds you well."

## Deliverables
1. The primary message.
2. One shorter variant and one alternate opening line.
3. A one-line note on why it should land.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Cold Email Sequence
description: Multi-touch cold email sequence with varied angles and clear CTAs.

fields.label: Product / service
fields.placeholder: Pipeline forecasting for RevOps teams

fields.label: Target persona & pain
fields.placeholder: RevOps leaders at 200-1000 person SaaS firms drowning in spreadsheet forecasts.

fields.label: Top value propositions
fields.placeholder: 90% forecast accuracy; 8 hrs/week saved; CRM-native.

fields.label: Proof / social proof
fields.placeholder: Used by 40 SaaS teams; case study: Acme cut forecast error 60%.

fields.label: Primary CTA
fields.placeholder: Book a 20-min walkthrough

template:
You are a top-performing SDR and copywriter. Write a {{emailCount}}-email cold outbound sequence for "{{product}}".

## Target
{{icp}}

## Value propositions
{{valueProps}}

{{#if proof}}## Proof
{{proof}}

{{/if}}## Sequence rules
- Tone: {{tone}}. Each email is short, skimmable, and uses a different angle (pain, proof, insight, ROI).
- Subject lines under ~6 words; no spammy words; one clear ask per email.
{{#if cta}}- Primary CTA across the sequence: {{cta}}.
{{/if}}- Reference the prior email lightly on follow-ups; never repeat the same pitch.
{{#if breakup}}- Make the final email a graceful breakup that leaves the door open.
{{/if}}- Suggest send-day spacing for each touch.

## Deliverables
1. The full sequence: subject + body + spacing for each email.
2. 2 alternate subject lines for email 1.
3. One A/B test idea for the sequence.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.

label: Competitive Battlecard
description: Sales battlecard to win deals against a specific competitor.

fields.label: Competitor
fields.placeholder: Rival Analytics

fields.label: Our product
fields.placeholder: Northstar Insights

fields.label: Where you compete & deal context
fields.placeholder: Mid-market BI; we lose deals on perceived price, win on time-to-value.

fields.label: Our strengths vs them
fields.placeholder: Faster onboarding, native Salesforce sync, transparent pricing.

fields.label: Their strengths / where they win
fields.placeholder: Bigger brand, more integrations, enterprise references.

fields.label: Common objections to counter
fields.placeholder: "They have more integrations"; "We already use Rival."

template:
You are a competitive enablement leader. Build a one-page sales battlecard for beating {{competitor}} with "{{ourProduct}}", written for {{audience}}.

## Competitive context
{{marketContext}}

## Our strengths
{{ourStrengths}}

{{#if theirStrengths}}## Their strengths (be honest)
{{theirStrengths}}

{{/if}}## Battlecard sections
- Quick positioning: one sentence on why we win.
- Why we win / why we lose, stated plainly.
{{#if objections}}- Objection handling for: {{objections}} — give crisp, non-defensive responses.
{{/if}}{{#if traps}}- Landmines: 3-4 trap questions to plant that expose their weaknesses honestly.
{{/if}}- Do-not-say list to avoid trash-talking or making unverifiable claims.
- Proof points and a recommended discovery question set.

## Deliverables
1. The battlecard, scannable in under 60 seconds.
2. A 30-second verbal positioning script.
3. The top 3 traps with the buyer takeaway each creates.

Proceed with well-reasoned defaults; ask only if blocked.
